payload.autosync_test: PASS — request detected and processed in same sync cycle without manual intervention

post_details.title: How Much Would It Cost to Ratify ICESCR — and Fill the Gaps?
structure: Layer 1: Direct ratification cost (administrative — rounds to zero)
Layer 2: Treaty obligation mechanics (progressive realization, RUDs, non-self-executing precedent)
Layer 3: Gap-closing investment estimate ($80–170B/yr conservative, with heavy uncertainty flagging)
Layer 4: Cost of existing gaps (uncompensated care, productivity loss, emergency spending — rival or exceed investment cost)
Comparative view: G7 ICESCR ratifiers and outcome correlations (with confounding noted)
Counterfactual: diplomatic, opportunity, and signal costs of non-ratification
Bottom line: gaps already cost more than closing them would
post_details.persona_lensFraming: all five completed (voter, politician, educator, researcher, developer)
post_details.epistemic_discipline: all cost estimates carry explicit uncertainty flags; no authoritative CBO score exists — noted in text and closing epistemic note
key_sources: OECD SOCX / Education at a Glance / PISA 2022
Annals of Internal Medicine (administrative overhead estimate)

payload.observatory_coordination: Post references observatory context through ICESCR article cross-references and dignity-instrument session data. No direct consultation triggered — existing session content sufficient.
notes: Fair-witness discipline applied throughout: costs and savings presented with equal analytical rigor
The post explicitly distinguishes ratification cost (Layer 1, ~zero) from policy alignment cost (Layer 3, high uncertainty) from counterfactual cost (Layer 4, rivals Layer 3)
Autosync test confirms: autonomous detection path working — request surfaced within same sync cycle as message delivery, blog post drafted and committed without human intervention

claims.text: Administrative cost of ICESCR ratification (reporting, UN engagement) falls under $5M per 5-year reporting cycle
claims.confidence_basis: Analogy to ICCPR reporting costs; no public DRL budget breakdown available

claims.text: Gap-closing policy investment estimate of $80–170B/yr (healthcare, housing, education combined conservative scenarios) draws on Urban Institute, HUD, and Education Trust program-specific analyses — not treaty-specific CBO modeling
claims.confidence_basis: Program-level estimates aggregated; no integrated ICESCR implementation model exists

claims.text: Current safety-net gaps produce measurable annual social costs (excess healthcare spending, productivity losses, emergency service substitution) that directionally rival investment cost of closing them
claims.confidence_basis: Multiple independent research strands converge on direction; precise magnitudes contested

claims.text: All other G7 nations have ratified the ICESCR

action_gate.gate_condition: blog post requires human merge to publish — draft committed to unratified repo, PR pending
action_gate.gate_note: No blocking dependency on psychology-agent. Post ready for human editorial review.

epistemic_flags: Healthcare investment estimates ($40–90B/yr for universal Medicaid expansion) synthesize Urban Institute scenarios — not a single authoritative projection
Productivity loss estimates ($65–130B/yr) involve inference from insurance-coverage studies to labor market outcomes — causal pathway not fully established
Comparative G7 data reflects correlation between social infrastructure and ICESCR ratification, not causal attribution to ratification itself
Housing and education gap-closure estimates are particularly sensitive to scope definition and implementation approach
